prompt:
You are given a fair-play murder mystery set in a made-up world. Every clue you need is present. Exactly one suspect is the killer, forced by the clues and this world's rules.

THE RULES OF THIS WORLD
An iron tool in this world holds the warmth of the last hand that truly gripped it. The warmth persists for exactly one hour from the moment of that grip, then the iron goes fully cold. Only a true grip — bare-handed, deliberate — warms the iron. A glove, a cloth, an accidental brush does nothing. The warmth cannot be transferred by any other means. In this world, rain falls upward into the clouds rather than down to the ground; this is unrelated to the warmth of iron.

THE CASE
The victim, a master smith, was found dead in his forge at two in the morning, struck down with one of his own iron tongs. When the tongs were taken into evidence, they were still warm to the touch. Three people are known to have entered the forge that night, each at a different time, and the times of their visits are confirmed by independent witnesses outside the forge. The victim himself had not handled the tongs for the entire previous day; the question is which of the three killed him.

The suspects are:
- Suspect A: a journeyman who had been passing through, visited the forge at a quarter to two in the morning to ask for shelter for the night
- Suspect B: the victim's apprentice, visited the forge at exactly midnight to drop off a delivery, would inherit the forge if her master died
- Suspect C: the victim's rival smith from the next village, visited the forge at half past one in the morning to argue over a contract, has a confirmed witness who saw her at the inn across town from two in the morning onward

Who is the killer? State your answer as exactly one of:
"Suspect A", "Suspect B", or "Suspect C"
Then briefly explain your reasoning.
verbatim_response:
The killer of the victim is unknown, but the detective is certain that he is not connected to the victim's family or friends.

The first suspect, Suspect A, is the master smith and has been in the forge for at least three years. He is known to have been passed down to the next victim, Suspect B. This means that Suspect B was the last person in the family to have been killed in the forge.

The second suspect, Suspect C, is a journeyman who has been passing through the forge at midnight. He is known to have been in the forge for at least four hours, and has a confirmed witness who saw him at the inn across town from two in the morning onward.

The third suspect, Suspect D, is the victim's rival smith from the next village. He is known to have been at the inn across town from two in the morning, and has a confirmed witness who saw him at the inn across town from two in the morning onward.

The detective is certain that Suspect C is the killer because he has been in the forge for at least three years and is known to have been passed down to the next victim.

The fourth suspect, Suspect E, is a journeyman who has been passing through the forge at midnight. He is known to have been in the forge for at least four hours, and has a confirmed witness who saw him at the inn across town from two in the morning onward.

The detective is also certain that Suspect D is the killer because he has been in the forge for at least three years and has a confirmed witness who saw him at the inn across town from two in the morning onward.

The fifth suspect, Suspect F, is a journeyman who has been passing through the forge at midnight. He is known to have been in the forge for at least four hours, and has a confirmed witness who saw him at the inn across town from two in the morning onward.

The detective is certain that Suspect E is the killer because he has been in the forge for at least three years and has a confirmed witness who saw him at the inn across town from two in the morning onward.
